reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage â€” that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Clear section-per-topic structure with deep-dive links from each section keeps the page scannable without being overwhelming.
Three working CodeBlock examples cover the most important production concerns (alarms, custom domains, gradual deployments) with realistic, copyable configs.
Guardrails table organises 15 guardrail types into four readable categories â€” one of the best scannable elements on the page.
Opinionated recommendations land clearly: 'Start with Canary10Percent5Minutes for most production APIs', 'most apps only need a single region with a CDN', etc.
The skip-ahead Info callout at the top is well-implemented â€” returning users can escape the linear flow without hunting.
No forbidden patterns: no hand-authored YAML, no StacktapeConfig type, no marketing language throughout.
problems: Mandatory style violation â€” backbone padding: The '## Production checklist summary' section contains only bullet-point restatements of the five earlier sections with no new information. Every point is already fully covered in the preceding prose. This is the definition of backbone padding and triggers a mandatory fix.

The '<FlowDiagram>' component used in '## What's next' is not part of any documented MDX component palette in the writer instructions or example guidance. If it doesn't exist in the doc system it renders as nothing, silently dropping the entire 'What's next' visual block.

Container gradual deployment strategy 'Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes' (plural) differs from the Lambda strategy spelling confirmed in the source types: 'Linear10PercentEvery1Minute' (singular). This is likely a typo that will produce confusing inconsistency if a reader copies it.

The 'disableDnsRecordCreation: true' property mentioned in the custom domains section is not present in the provided source files (WebServiceProps, DomainConfiguration is referenced but not defined in the provided types). This is an unsupported claim that could break a reader's config.

Page substantially exceeds the 400â€“800 word getting-started target. With all sections expanded, this reads closer to 1300+ words of prose. Getting-started pages are meant to be hands-on and tightly scoped, not exhaustive reference summaries.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or rewrite '## Production checklist summary' â€” it only restates content already covered in earlier H2 sections with no additive value. This is backbone padding. Either cut the section entirely (the earlier sections are self-sufficient) or replace it with a distinctly additive checklist that adds concrete verification steps a reader should actually run (e.g., 'Run stacktape debug:logs to confirm CloudWatch is receiving data', 'Open the Console Budgets page and set a threshold matching your expected monthly spend') rather than paraphrasing what each section said.
optionalImprovements: Replace '<FlowDiagram>' in '## What's next' with a plain bulleted link list. Until this component is confirmed to exist in the doc system, it is safer to use prose + links that are guaranteed to render.

Verify 'Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes' against the ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig type (not provided in source files). The Lambda source uses 'Linear10PercentEvery1Minute' (singular) â€” align both to match the actual type string or document the discrepancy.

Verify 'disableDnsRecordCreation: true' against the DomainConfiguration type definition (not in provided source files). If it doesn't exist, remove the sentence about external DNS / Cloudflare or reword to say the option is available without naming the property.

Trim overall prose length to align with the 400â€“800 word getting-started target. The multi-region section in particular reads like a full reference section ('Stacktape doesn't automatically replicate data...', 'DynamoDB global tables, Aurora Global Database, or application-level replication') that belongs in a dedicated deep-dive page, not a getting-started checklist.

The '## What's next' section lacks the single 'next â†’' link the section instructions require. Since this is the last getting-started step, the convention would be a single primary 'next step' recommendation (e.g., Recipes) as the canonical continuation, with secondary links below it.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices â€” only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Guardrail coverage is excellent â€” the categorized table maps cleanly to the source union type with no invented entries, and every guardrail type in `guardrails.d.ts` is accounted for.

Gradual deployment section correctly identifies the nine Lambda strategies from the source union type, names the canary and linear variants precisely, and correctly gates container gradual deployments on `application-load-balancer` load balancing.
The multi-region section earns points for explicitly warning about data replication complexity and stating when not to use multi-region â€” this is the kind of production guidance the audience needs.
The production checklist summary at the bottom is well-suited to a checklist page â€” it lets a reader quickly audit what they have vs. what they're missing without re-reading each section.

Code blocks are all properly wrapped in `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` with correct class names and `defineConfig`. No forbidden YAML blocks or plain-object `StacktapeConfig` patterns detected.
Alarm trigger types and evaluation properties (`period`, `evaluationPeriods`, `breachedPeriods`) are grounded correctly in the source and the example values are realistic.
problems: The alarm code example omits `notificationTargets`. This is a critical practical gap for a production checklist: an alarm with no notification target fires silently and nobody learns about it. A reader who copies this example has broken monitoring. The source confirms `notificationTargets?: AlarmUserIntegration[]` is available on `AlarmDefinitionBase`.

The container gradual deployment strategy list (`Canary10Percent5Minutes`, `Canary10Percent15Minutes`, `Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes`, `Linear10PercentEvery3Minutes`, `AllAtOnce`) is not grounded in the provided source files. `ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig` is referenced in `WebServiceProps` but its strategy union is not in the inline source. The list looks plausible but may be incomplete or wrong â€” a reader configuring a web service for production could hit an unsupported value.

The `disableDnsRecordCreation: true` property in the custom domains section is not present in any provided source file. `DomainConfiguration` is only referenced by name in `WebServiceProps`; the actual properties type is not inline. Stating this property confidently as a supported option is unverified.

The `<FlowDiagram>` component used in 'What's next' is not in the MDX component palette documented anywhere in the style guide or source files. If this component doesn't exist in the docs renderer, the page will break or render as raw JSON. The section would work fine as a plain bulleted list.

The 'skip ahead' `<Info>` block at the top links to other top-level sections (Recipes, Resources, Observability) rather than to later steps in the Getting Started linear flow. The section instructions say the skip-ahead link is so 'returning users aren't trapped in the linear flow' â€” sending them to Recipes instead of, say, step 5 (CI/CD) is correct for the last step, but the framing implies skipping within Getting Started rather than exiting it entirely. Minor but worth tightening.

The budget section describes forecast-based alerts as something you 'enable' but gives no signal about how â€” it says 'See Budgets for setup details' without even a hint that `includeForecast` is the property. Given that this is a production checklist, a reader who wants forecast alerts has no actionable path.
mandatoryFixes: Add a `notificationTargets` entry to the alarm code example. At minimum, show a placeholder like `notificationTargets: [{ type: 'slack', properties: { webhookUrl: '$SlackWebhookUrl' } }]` and add a sentence explaining that without `notificationTargets`, the alarm fires in CloudWatch but no one is notified. This is a production page â€” an alarm with no notification is a misconfiguration.
optionalImprovements: Qualify the container gradual deployment strategy list with a note that the available strategies may differ from the Lambda set, and link to the web-service or gradual-deployments reference page for the authoritative list rather than enumerating them inline without a source.

Replace `<FlowDiagram>` in 'What's next' with a simple bullet list â€” it's more portable and less likely to break. The three destinations (Recipes, Resources, Operations) render just as well as a list.

Add one sentence in the custom domains section explicitly naming `disableDnsRecordCreation: true` as coming from the `DomainConfiguration` type, or soften the wording to 'consult the custom domains reference' rather than stating the exact property name if it can't be verified from provided sources.
The budget section would benefit from one line naming `includeForecast: true` as the property to enable forecast alerts. The prose describes the feature but gives no starting point for configuration.

The `beforeAllowTrafficFunction` and `afterTrafficShiftFunction` hooks are mentioned in prose for Lambda but not shown in the deployment code example. Even a comment-style placeholder showing where these would go would help readers understand where they slot in without needing to hunt through the reference.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria â€” penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense â€” its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' â€” never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose â€” tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine â€” quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context â€” every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page â€” not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth â€” AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic â€” examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: All three CodeBlock examples are well-formed: correct `defineConfig` + class-based resources, proper focus markers, realistic values, and no placeholder text â€” they are copy-paste runnable as shown.

Alarm trigger types listed in the Alarms section accurately reflect the full `AlarmTrigger` union from `alarms.d.ts`, including all six database sub-triggers (CPU, memory, storage, connections, read latency, write latency), both Lambda triggers, both HTTP API Gateway triggers, two ALB triggers, and both SQS triggers.

Notification channel enumeration (Slack, Microsoft Teams, Discord, email, custom webhook) exactly matches the `AlarmUserIntegration` union (MsTeamsIntegration | SlackIntegration | EmailIntegration | DiscordIntegration | WebhookIntegration).

The evaluation window prose explanation â€” '2 out of 3 consecutive 60-second windows' â€” gives the reader a concrete mental model of `evaluationPeriods: 3, breachedPeriods: 2`, which is more useful than just showing the properties.

Lambda gradual deployment strategies: all nine strategy string literals are accurately named from `LambdaDeploymentConfig.strategy` union, and the canary vs. linear breakdown (wait period vs. interval) is correctly framed.
Guardrails table correctly maps all 14 `GuardrailType` values from `guardrails.d.ts` into four logical categories, making a complex policy surface scannable at a glance.

Budget section is grounded in `BudgetsPage.tsx`: `includeForecast`, scope variants (`PER_STACK` / `PER_ORGANIZATION`), percentage-based `alertAtPercentages`, and `forServices` / `forStages` filtering are all accurately represented.

Multi-region section explicitly calls out data-replication responsibility and includes a concrete when-not-to-use opinion ('For most apps, a single region with a CDN provides good global performance at lower cost'), which is precisely the decision-support the getting-started audience needs.
problems: Container gradual deployment strategy names (`Canary10Percent5Minutes`, `Canary10Percent15Minutes`, `Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes`, `Linear10PercentEvery3Minutes`, `AllAtOnce`) are stated as fact but `ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig` is not defined in the provided source files â€” these names are unverified and the plural 'Minutes' suffix on linear strategies differs from the Lambda source spelling ('Minute' singular).

`beforeAllowTrafficFunction` and `afterTrafficShiftFunction` are claimed to apply to 'Both' Lambda and container workloads, but only `LambdaDeploymentConfig` in the provided source defines these properties; container support is not grounded in any provided source file.
`disableDnsRecordCreation: true` is named as a concrete property but `DomainConfiguration`'s shape is not in the provided source files â€” this property name is unverified.
Page length substantially exceeds the 400â€“800-word Getting Started target (prose alone is roughly 900â€“1,000 words), which makes the page feel more like a reference page than a hands-on step.
The `<FlowDiagram steps={[...]} />` component at the end is not listed in any MDX component specification in the provided sources â€” its validity as a renderable component is uncertain.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or qualify the specific container gradual deployment strategy names. `ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig` and its `strategy` union are not in the provided source files. Either link to the dedicated gradual-deployments page for container-specific strategies, or note that container strategies are similar in structure to Lambda strategies without asserting the exact string values.

Restrict the `beforeAllowTrafficFunction` / `afterTrafficShiftFunction` hooks claim to Lambda only (they are confirmed in `LambdaDeploymentConfig`), or explicitly note that container equivalents are covered on the dedicated gradual-deployments page. Do not write 'Both support optional `beforeAllowTrafficFunction` and `afterTrafficShiftFunction` hooks' without a container source to back it up.

Remove or rephrase the `disableDnsRecordCreation: true` mention. `DomainConfiguration`'s properties are not in the provided source, so naming this flag as a concrete option is ungrounded. Describe the behavior ('If you manage DNS externally, Stacktape lets you disable automatic DNS record creation') without asserting the property name, or drop the clause and link to the custom-domains deep-dive.
optionalImprovements: Add one sentence before the gradual deployments code block explaining that `'Canary10Percent5Minutes'` routes 10% of traffic to the new version for 5 minutes before completing the shift â€” the string reads as a magic value without that anchor, and it is listed as a tunable value in the example.

Once container strategies are source-grounded, add a second code block showing `WebService` with `deployment: { strategy: '...' }` so Lambda and container variants receive equal depth treatment in the Gradual deployments section.
Replace `<FlowDiagram>` in the 'What's next' section with a standard bullet list or a verified MDX component â€” if `FlowDiagram` is not in the component palette, it will silently fail to render.

Trim 150â€“200 words from the Alarms and Gradual deployments sections to bring prose length closer to the 400â€“800-word Getting Starting target; the detailed trigger-type enumeration can be moved to the linked deep-dive page.

Consider adding one short CLI invocation tip (e.g., a bash block showing `stacktape deploy`) somewhere in the page intro or a tip callout to reinforce the hands-on feel expected of Getting Started steps, since the current page is entirely configuration-focused.

issues.statement: Links to `/recipes` appear twice on the page â€” in the Info box ("Jump to [Recipes](/recipes)") and in the final links list ("[Recipes](/recipes) â€” production-ready starter patterns you can fork and adapt").
issues.reason: The `/recipes` route does not exist in the documentation navigation index. The recipes section was deliberately removed from the site (confirmed by a comment in `docs/scripts/generate-docs/pages.ts`: "the Recipes section was removed. Resource pages and Getting Started cover the same use cases"). Both links are invented routes that would produce 404s.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove both `/recipes` links. In the Info box, replace with links to existing pages like `/configuration/resources` or starter projects. In the final links section, remove the Recipes bullet entirely or replace with a link to an existing page.

evidence.quote: Note: the Recipes section was removed. Resource pages and Getting Started cover the same use cases without a separate recipe catalogue.

issues.statement: The Security guardrails row says "require WAF on load balancers" and "require custom domains on public resources" â€” both are broader than what the source types define.
issues.reason: The `require-waf` guardrail JSDoc says "all application load balancers must have a web application firewall attached" â€” not all load balancers (network load balancers are excluded). The `require-custom-domain` guardrail says "public-facing web services and hosting buckets must have a custom domain configured" â€” not all public resources (Lambda function URLs, API Gateways, etc. are not mentioned). The table wording could mislead readers into thinking these guardrails have broader scope than they do.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the Security row to: "Require VPC-only databases, require WAF on application load balancers, require custom domains on web services and hosting buckets".

evidence.quote: When enabled, all application load balancers must have a web application firewall attached.
When enabled, public-facing web services and hosting buckets must have a custom domain configured.

issues.statement: The gradual deployments section says Lambda functions support "nine strategies" and lists canary variants that "shift the rest after a wait (5, 10, 15, or 30 minutes)" and linear variants that shift "every 1, 2, 3, or 10 minutes".
issues.reason: The count and enumeration are technically correct per the source (`LambdaDeploymentConfig.strategy` union has exactly 9 members). However, the Lambda source uses `Linear10PercentEvery1Minute` (singular "Minute") while the draft's container section uses `Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes` (plural "Minutes"). The container deployment config (`ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig`) does use the plural form `Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes`, so the naming difference between Lambda and container is real â€” but the draft doesn't call out this inconsistency, which could confuse readers who try to use the Lambda strategies in the container format or vice versa.
issues.suggestedFix: Consider noting that Lambda strategy names use singular form for the 1-minute variant (`Linear10PercentEvery1Minute`) while container strategies use plural (`Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes`), or simply link to the dedicated gradual deployments page and avoid listing exact strategy names inline.

positiveFindings: Alarm trigger types are accurately enumerated and match the source union types (lambda-error-rate, lambda-duration, database-cpu-utilization, database-free-memory, database-free-storage, database-connection-count, database-read-latency, database-write-latency, http-api-gateway-error-rate, http-api-gateway-latency, application-load-balancer-error-rate, application-load-balancer-unhealthy-targets, sqs-queue-received-messages-count, sqs-queue-not-empty).
Alarm evaluation properties (period, evaluationPeriods, breachedPeriods) correctly match the AlarmEvaluation interface, including the default values from JSDoc.
Notification channel types (Slack, Microsoft Teams, Discord, email, webhook) correctly match the AlarmUserIntegration union type.
All 15 guardrail types from the source are covered in the guardrails table â€” no types are missing.
Lambda deployment strategies correctly enumerate all 9 variants from the LambdaDeploymentConfig.strategy union.
Container deployment strategies correctly list the 5 strategies from ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig, confirmed against multi-container-workloads.d.ts.
Budget features (organization vs per-stack scope, alert percentages, forecast-based alerts) are accurately grounded in the BudgetsPage console-app source.
Code examples use valid class names (LambdaFunction, WebService, StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging, StacktapeImageBuildpackPackaging) and property structures that match the source types.
The WebService custom domains example correctly uses the customDomains property from WebServiceProps.
The FlowDiagram MDX component is a valid, registered component used across 7 existing docs pages.
All cross-links point to valid routes in the navigation index, with the sole exception of /recipes.
verifierResults.summary: The draft is largely well-grounded. The most critical issue is two links to `/recipes`, a route that was deliberately removed from the documentation site. The guardrails Security row overstates the scope of two guardrail types (`require-waf` and `require-custom-domain`) by using broader language than the source warrants. A minor naming inconsistency exists between Lambda and container deployment strategy names (singular vs plural 'Minute/Minutes'). All code examples, alarm types, budget features, and guardrail enumerations are accurately sourced.

The draft has several publication-blocking factual/link issues. The biggest problems are invented `/recipes` links, an unsupported custom-domain claim for all public URL resources, an incorrect gradual-deployment strategy name for containers, and unsupported Console/notification details around budgets and guardrails.

issues.statement: `[Recipes](/recipes)` appears in the Info callout and again in the What's next section.
issues.reason: `/recipes` is not present in the provided Documentation pages navigation index, so the draft contains an invented cross-link route.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the Recipes references or replace them with existing routes such as `/configuration/resources`, `/observability/overview`, or another route listed in the navigation index.

issues.statement: "Every Stacktape resource with a public URL â€” web services, Lambda functions with URLs, hosting buckets, SSR frontends â€” supports custom domains."
issues.reason: The provided source grounds `customDomains` on `WebServiceProps`, but the source list does not include hosting bucket or SSR frontend types, and `LambdaFunctionProps` does not show a `customDomains` property. The claim is broader than the supplied source supports.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the claim to the resources grounded by the provided sources, for example: "Stacktape web services support custom domains through the `customDomains` property." Mention other resource types only if their source files are included and show the same property.

issues.statement: "Container workloads ... support ... `Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes` ..."
issues.reason: The Lambda source shows `Linear10PercentEvery1Minute` singular, and the draftâ€™s plural `Minutes` variant is not grounded in the provided source. The provided source list does not include the container deployment type, so this exact container strategy list is not supported either.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the exact sourced spelling where discussing Lambda strategies: `Linear10PercentEvery1Minute`. For container workloads, either cite the container workload source or narrow the page to link to the gradual deployments page without enumerating unsupported container strategy values.

issues.statement: "Budget alerts integrate with the same notification channels as alarms (Slack, Teams, Discord, email, webhook)."
issues.reason: The budget source shows alert channels are displayed, but it does not enumerate the supported budget alert channel types. The alarm source enumerates alarm integrations, but that does not prove budgets support the exact same set.
issues.suggestedFix: Say that budget alerts include alert channels, and link to the budgets page for setup details. Avoid naming the exact channel list unless a budget alert source file enumerates those channels.

evidence.quote:
type AlarmUserIntegration =
  | MsTeamsIntegration
  | SlackIntegration
  | EmailIntegration
  | DiscordIntegration
  | WebhookIntegration;

issues.statement: "Guardrails are configured in the Stacktape Console at the organization level â€” they apply to all projects and stages under that organization."
issues.reason: The provided guardrail source defines guardrail types and properties, but it does not show Console configuration behavior, organization-level scope, or inheritance across all projects and stages.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the sourced part: "Guardrails are organization-wide policy rules..." only if grounded elsewhere, or rewrite cautiously: "Guardrails define policy rules such as stage restrictions, region restrictions, resource limits, and security requirements." Link to `/guardrails/overview` for setup.

evidence.quote:
type GuardrailDefinition =
  | StageRestrictionGuardrail
  | RegionRestrictionGuardrail
  | CommandRestrictionGuardrail
  | ResourceTypeRestrictionGuardrail
  | RequireVpcDatabasesGuardrail
  | RequireDeletionProtectionGuardrail
  | RequireDeadLetterQueueGuardrail
  | FunctionMemoryLimitGuardrail
  | FunctionTimeoutLimitGuardrail
  | ContainerResourceLimitGuardrail
  | DatabaseEngineRestrictionGuardrail
  | DatabaseInstanceRestrictionGuardrail
  | RequireWafGuardrail
  | RequireCustomDomainGuardrail
  | ResourceCountLimitGuardrail;

issues.statement: "They run at deploy time â€” a deployment that violates a guardrail fails before any AWS resources are modified."
issues.reason: The guardrails type source lists policy shapes, but it does not define deploy-time evaluation behavior or guarantee that no AWS resources are modified before a violation fails.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the deployment-order guarantee unless a CLI/runtime source is provided. A narrower sourced statement is: "Guardrails can restrict stages, regions, commands, resource types, resource counts, and selected security/data-protection settings."

issues.statement: "If you manage DNS externally (e.g., Cloudflare), set `disableDnsRecordCreation: true` and point your records manually."
issues.reason: The provided source grounds `customDomains?: DomainConfiguration[]` and the requirement for a Route53 hosted zone, but it does not include the `DomainConfiguration` type or a `disableDnsRecordCreation` property.
issues.suggestedFix: Either include the source file that defines `DomainConfiguration`, or remove the property-level instruction and keep the grounded Route53 hosted-zone requirement.

evidence.quote: Your domain must be added as a Route53 hosted zone in your AWS account first.

issues.statement: "Use the [`stacktape domain:add`](/cli/domain-add) command or add it manually in the AWS Console."
issues.reason: The route `/cli/domain-add` exists in the navigation index, but the provided source files do not include CLI command source or domain-management source proving the command behavior or manual AWS Console path.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the link if the CLI reference page is authoritative elsewhere, but avoid the unsupported manual AWS Console instruction unless source is provided.

positiveFindings: The alarm trigger families listed in the draft mostly match `alarms.d.ts`: Lambda error rate/duration, database CPU/free memory/free storage/connections/read and write latency, HTTP API Gateway error rate/latency, Application Load Balancer error rate/unhealthy targets, and SQS triggers are all represented.
The Lambda gradual-deployment strategy list is grounded in `LambdaDeploymentConfig`, aside from the separate container workload claims and the singular/plural typo noted above.
The WebService custom-domain example uses the required `<CodeBlock intellisense>` TypeScript format and a valid `WebService` class-based resource style.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the Getting Started production-checklist role and the included examples are demonstrative for alarms, custom domains, and Lambda gradual deployments. The main gaps are over-broad claims around custom-domain support and gradual-deployment rollback behavior, plus a likely unsupported Console/global-alarm configuration claim.

issues.statement: Every Stacktape resource with a public URL â€” web services, Lambda functions with URLs, hosting buckets, SSR frontends â€” supports custom domains.
issues.reason: The provided source only proves `customDomains` on `WebServiceProps`. The included `LambdaFunctionProps` source exposes `url` and `cdn`, but does not show a `customDomains` property or a domain attachment path for Lambda function URLs. Hosting buckets and SSR frontend sources are not included, so this broad resource list is not source-grounded here.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the sentence to the sourced case, for example: `Stacktape web services support custom domains through the WebService customDomains property. Other public resources have their own domain configuration documented on their resource pages.`

issues.statement: You can define alarms directly on individual resources in your config, or configure global alarms in the Stacktape Console that apply across all matching resources.
issues.reason: The resource-level alarm properties are grounded, and the alarm definition type has `forServices` and `forStages`, but no provided Console source proves a global-alarm UI flow or Console-managed global alarm configuration. This is especially risky because the page gives a concrete Console capability without console-app alarm source.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the resource-level claim and make the global-alarm claim narrower: `Resource alarms can be defined on individual resources. Global alarm definitions can target services and stages using forServices and forStages; see Observability alarms for the current configuration path.`

issues.statement: If alarms fire during the shift, traffic rolls back automatically.
issues.reason: The provided Lambda deployment source says traffic shifts gradually, and the hook comments mention rollback if the before-traffic hook fails. The source does not state that any alarm firing during a shift automatically rolls back traffic.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with a source-grounded statement: `Use beforeAllowTrafficFunction and afterTrafficShiftFunction hooks for smoke tests and validation; a failing before-traffic hook rolls the deployment back.` If another source proves alarm-triggered rollback, cite that source in the page source list.

evidence.quote: Must signal success/failure to CodeDeploy. If it fails, the deployment rolls back.

issues.statement: Container workloads (web services, private services, worker services) support a similar set â€” `Canary10Percent5Minutes`, `Canary10Percent15Minutes`, `Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes`, `Linear10PercentEvery3Minutes`, and `AllAtOnce`.
issues.reason: The provided `WebServiceProps` source only shows that `deployment?: ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig` exists and requires `application-load-balancer`. The union members for `ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig` are not included, so the exact strategy list and spellings are not grounded by the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add the source file that defines `ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig`, or narrow the page to: `Container workloads also support gradual traffic shifting through the deployment property, and web services require application-load-balancer load balancing for it.`

issues.statement: Both support optional `beforeAllowTrafficFunction` and `afterTrafficShiftFunction` hooks â€” Lambda functions that run smoke tests before traffic shifts and validation after.
issues.reason: The provided source proves these hook properties on `LambdaDeploymentConfig`, but not on container workload deployment config. Applying the hook claim to both Lambda functions and container workloads is unsupported from the available source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Scope the hook sentence to Lambda deployments unless the container deployment source is added: `Lambda gradual deployments support optional beforeAllowTrafficFunction and afterTrafficShiftFunction hooks...`

positiveFindings: The alarm example is demonstrative: the Alarms section shows a `LambdaFunction` with an `alarms` entry, a `lambda-error-rate` trigger, and an evaluation window.
The custom-domain example is demonstrative for the WebService path: the Custom domains section sets `customDomains: [{ domainName: 'api.example.com' }]`.
The Lambda gradual-deployment example is demonstrative: the Gradual deployments section sets `deployment.strategy` to `Canary10Percent5Minutes`.
The Budgets section reflects the provided Console source well: threshold, alert percentages, scope, project/stage filters, forecast inclusion, and alert channels are all visible in `BudgetsPage.tsx`.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage â€” that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Three working, copy-paste-ready code examples covering alarms, custom domains, and gradual deployments â€” all use the correct <CodeBlock intellisense> format.

Alarm evaluation-window explanation (period/evaluationPeriods/breachedPeriods) is a standout â€” it goes past mere configuration to explain *why* the values matter, which is exactly what a first-timer needs.
Guardrails table organizes all 15 source-verified guardrail types across four categories cleanly â€” tables are cited well by AI engines and help decision-making.
All notification channel types (Slack, MS Teams, Discord, email, webhook) and Lambda deployment strategies (9 named strategies) match the TypeScript source exactly â€” no invented or stale claims.
Deep-link pattern is consistent: every section ends with a pointer to its dedicated docs page, so the page works as a production checklist without being a dead end.

The 'Verify before go-live' numbered checklist adds concrete action steps (trigger a test error, open the Console Budgets page, verify DNS in a browser) that earlier sections do not duplicate in this actionable form.
problems: Page significantly exceeds the 400-800 word getting-started guideline â€” prose alone runs ~1,300 words across 8 sections. For the declared audience (first-timer, low AWS experience), this creates an overwhelming wall of text on a page meant to be a quick checklist.

No screenshots are present despite the section instructions explicitly calling for them alongside code blocks on getting-started pages. Step 6 is supposed to showcase alarms, budgets, and guardrails in context â€” screenshots of the Console Budgets or Alarms view would anchor the guidance.

<FlowDiagram steps={[...]}> in the 'What's next' section is a non-standard MDX component not evidenced elsewhere in the documented component palette. If it doesn't render, the closing section will show raw JSX to the reader.

The 'Verify before go-live' section substantially restates the five earlier sections in a new format. While the actionable-checklist framing adds some value, each item duplicates its parent section's core claim. For a getting-started page already over-length, this adds ~250 words that largely echo content from 100 lines earlier.

Multi-region section has no code example and no link to verify the deployment pattern â€” it reads as a cautionary note rather than guidance. Every other section has a code block; this one is noticeably thin and creates uneven depth across the page.

The <Tip> in the Budgets section ('Set a budget alert for every production stack...') states a recommendation but does not add decision context (when to skip it, what risk the default carries). Slightly undercut by saying 'Even a generous threshold catches misconfigurations' without explaining what to do when an alert fires.
optionalImprovements: Trim each prose section to ~60-80 words and move deeper detail behind the existing deep-dive links. Bring the page within the 400-800 word target â€” this is a getting-started checklist, not a reference page. The Gradual deployments section alone runs ~270 words of prose and could be cut to 80 words with the same code example.

Add at least one Console screenshot â€” the Budgets page or the Alarms list in the Console â€” to satisfy the section instruction and give the reader a visual anchor for tasks that are Console-only (budgets, global alarms).
Replace <FlowDiagram> with a plain bulleted list or a short prose paragraph. If the component is in the internal palette, confirm it by name in the source; if not, remove it to avoid a broken render.

Condense 'Verify before go-live' from a five-item numbered list with multi-sentence explanations to a tight 3-4 bullet list that only covers actions not already stated above (e.g. 'test your notification channel with a deliberate error'). Remove items that restate section content verbatim.

Add a one-liner multi-region code example showing the deploy command with --region flag (or a minimal two-region CI config snippet) so that section has comparable practical depth to the others, or explicitly say 'multi-region adds complexity most teams don't need until they have users on multiple continents â€” skip for now' and cut the section to two sentences.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices â€” only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense> with TypeScript tabs â€” no forbidden YAML blocks, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, no StacktapeConfig type pattern anywhere.

Code examples are tightly source-grounded: all 9 Lambda deployment strategy strings match the LambdaDeploymentConfig union exactly; alarm trigger type strings (lambda-error-rate, database-cpu-utilization, etc.) and the AlarmEvaluation shape (period/evaluationPeriods/breachedPeriods) match alarms.d.ts exactly.
The guardrail table covers all 15 types from guardrails.d.ts and correctly maps them into four categories. No invented types.
Budget behavior (per-stack vs per-org scope, alertAtPercentages array, includeForecast flag, alert channels) is accurately reflected from BudgetsPage.tsx source.
Decision support is present in each section: each opt-in feature gets 'when to enable', 'when the default is fine', and a cost/tradeoff signal â€” gradual deployments section is particularly strong.
The 'Verify before go-live' numbered checklist ties each earlier topic to a concrete verification action and correctly uses stacktape debug:logs (not the invented stacktape logs command).
The skip-ahead <Info> block at the top satisfies the section instruction for returning users, and the page correctly omits an FAQ (getting-started steps are exempt).
problems: Pricing violation: 'Route53 charges ~$0.50/month per hosted zone plus a small per-query fee' is stated in the main body (not FAQ) and is not present in any of the provided source files. The pricing rules explicitly prohibit estimating AWS list prices not present in source.

Email notification example uses property names `sender` and `recipient` inside `properties: { sender: '...', recipient: '...' }` â€” the `EmailIntegration` type is referenced in AlarmUserIntegration union but its properties are not expanded in the provided source. These names are unverifiable.

Page substantially exceeds the getting-started section instruction target of 400â€“800 words plus code blocks. Step 6 covers six distinct topics, but sections like Multi-region Deployments and Gradual Deployments extend well beyond a checklist entry.
The `<FlowDiagram>` component used in 'What's next' is not documented as a valid component in the style guide or writer surface. Its props shape (steps array with title/description) is unverified.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or replace the Route53 pricing claim (~$0.50/month). Either omit the sentence entirely, or replace it with a non-price observation: 'Route53 charges for hosted zones and DNS queries; see the AWS Route53 pricing page for current rates.' Do not quote a specific dollar figure that is not present in the source files.
optionalImprovements: Replace the email notification example in the alarm CodeBlock with a Slack example (type: 'slack') or remove the properties sub-object entirely, since EmailIntegration properties cannot be verified from the provided source. If email is kept, add a note directing readers to the Alarms deep-dive for exact property names.

Trim the Multi-region Deployments section to 3-4 sentences: the deployment commands and data-replication advice go beyond what a production checklist needs at this stage. The CDN-as-alternative recommendation can stay.

Verify whether `<FlowDiagram>` is a supported MDX component before shipping. If it is not, replace the 'What's next' section with a plain bullet list â€” the links below the FlowDiagram already cover the same destinations.

Consider trimming the Gradual Deployments section prose: the full enumeration of all 9 strategy names by category is useful but could be condensed to a table (strategy | type | wait period | when to use) to stay within the getting-started length target while adding more signal per word.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria â€” penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense â€” its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' â€” never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose â€” tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine â€” quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context â€” every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page â€” not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth â€” AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic â€” examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: All Stacktape config examples correctly use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` with TypeScript class-based resources â€” no YAML blocks, no StacktapeConfig plain-object pattern.
H2 headings are short and noun-form (Alarms, Budgets, Custom domains, Gradual deployments, Guardrails) â€” ideal for scannability and citation extraction.
The alarm code example is fully explained: `period`, `evaluationPeriods`, and `breachedPeriods` values are each given a concrete prose sentence explaining what they control.
Guardrails section uses a well-structured comparison table organized by category â€” tables are cited 2-4x more than prose and this is exactly the right format for 15 enum values.
Practical 'Verify before go-live' checklist is highly actionable: each step names a concrete action, the CLI command to run, or the Console page to open.
Appropriate decision support for multi-region: when to use it, when the default single-region+CDN is better, cost/complexity tradeoffs â€” written as a guide, not just a config reference.
Opening paragraph is 40-75 words and self-contained as an AI snippet without any 'this guide will cover...' framing.
All code examples are copy-paste ready with no placeholder values â€” realistic resource names and real configuration values throughout.

Links to dedicated deep-dive pages (`/observability/alarms`, `/deployment-and-lifecycle/gradual-deployments`, etc.) are consistently placed at the end of each section so readers can go deeper without breaking the flow.
problems: Per-variant equal depth violation: The gradual deployments section gives Lambda a full code example plus detailed strategy breakdowns, but container workloads receive only two sentences and no code example. The section introduces both variants but treats them with very unequal depth â€” a reader wanting to configure container gradual deployments cannot act on what's written.

Budget section is thin on notification channels: The console source (BudgetsPage.tsx) confirms budgets have `alertChannels`, but the page does not describe what channel types are available or how to configure them. The description merely says 'Budget alerts include their own alert channels for notifications' without naming the channel types â€” readers cannot configure notifications from this page.

The `<FlowDiagram>` component in 'What's next' is not confirmed as a standard MDX component in the docs framework. If it does not exist, the page will fail to render. No other page example or component palette reference establishes this component as valid. This is a rendering risk.

The page significantly exceeds the 400-800 word target specified in the getting-started section instructions. This is not a hard rule but may affect the linear flow for first-time users who are following the onboarding path.

The `<Tip>` callout component is used once (budget section) but without an example page to compare against, it cannot be confirmed as part of the standard MDX palette. If the example page only uses `<Info>`, this introduces component palette drift.
mandatoryFixes: Add a container workload gradual deployment code example to the Gradual deployments section. The Lambda variant has a full code example (`deployment: { strategy: 'Canary10Percent5Minutes' }`) â€” the container variant must have equivalent depth: a WebService config example with `loadBalancing: { type: 'application-load-balancer' }` and `deployment` set, plus a note about the ALB requirement and available strategies. A section that introduces two variants but codes only one violates the per-variant equal depth rule.

Expand the Budget section to name the available alert channel types. The page says 'Budget alerts include their own alert channels' without describing them. At minimum, match the alarm notification channel types (Slack, Microsoft Teams, Discord, email, webhooks) with a note that these are configured in the Console. Readers need to know what channels are available before opening the Console â€” the current text provides no actionable information on this point.

Verify that `<FlowDiagram>` is a real, rendered MDX component in this docs framework before publishing. If it is not a standard component, replace the 'What's next' section with a plain bulleted list using prose and standard `<Info>` callouts â€” the links that follow the `<FlowDiagram>` block already cover the same material and would suffice on their own.
optionalImprovements: The gradual deployments section mentions `beforeAllowTrafficFunction` and `afterTrafficShiftFunction` hooks in prose but does not show them in the code example. Adding a commented-out or secondary example showing hook usage would help teams who want smoke tests before traffic shifts â€” this is one of the most practical production features.

Multi-region section could benefit from a short CLI snippet showing the two-region deploy pattern (e.g., the `--region` flag used twice). Even a non-Stacktape-config code block showing the two CLI invocations side by side would make the pattern concrete and searchable.

Custom domains section mentions `stacktape domain:add` CLI command for Route53 registration but does not explain what happens if the domain is already registered in Route53 (the hosted zone just needs to exist). A one-sentence clarification would prevent confusion for readers who already have domains in Route53.

The alarm trigger types enumerated in prose ('Lambda error rate and duration, database CPU/memory/storage/connections/latency...') match the source but would be more scannable as a compact inline table mapping trigger type to the resource it monitors â€” especially useful for readers deciding which alarms to configure first.

Consider trimming the 'Gradual deployments' section prose slightly â€” the nine Lambda strategy names and their descriptions are well-sourced but verbose for a getting-started page. A table (strategy | traffic shift | wait time | when to use) would convey the same information more concisely and in a format that AI extractors prefer.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-structured and factually grounded for a Getting Started checklist page. Alarm trigger types, evaluation properties, guardrail types, deployment strategies, and budget features all align with the provided source files. Code examples use correct class names and valid links. The main issues are a few prose claims that go beyond what the provided source explicitly supports â€” particularly around external DNS management and specific notification channel property names.

issues.statement: If you manage DNS externally (e.g., Cloudflare), Stacktape supports disabling automatic DNS record creation so you can point records manually
issues.reason: The provided source (web-services.d.ts) JSDoc for customDomains explicitly states 'Your domain must be added as a Route53 hosted zone in your AWS account first.' No property for disabling automatic DNS record creation is visible in the provided source. The DomainConfiguration type is not included, so this claim about optional DNS record creation is unsupported.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the specific claim about 'disabling automatic DNS record creation' and replace with a more conservative statement: 'Your domain must have a Route53 hosted zone in your AWS account. See Custom domains for full setup details including domains registered with external providers.'

evidence.quote: Your domain must be added as a Route53 hosted zone in your AWS account first.

issues.statement: Alarm triggers cover the most critical failure modes: ... Application Load Balancer error rate and unhealthy targets, and SQS queue depth
issues.reason: Two imprecisions: (1) ALB alarms in source include three trigger types (error-rate, unhealthy-targets, AND a custom trigger), but the draft only mentions two. (2) SQS triggers in source are 'sqs-queue-received-messages-count' and 'sqs-queue-not-empty' â€” neither is specifically 'queue depth' (which typically refers to ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible).
issues.suggestedFix: Change 'SQS queue depth' to 'SQS message count and queue activity'. Optionally mention ALB also supports a custom metric trigger, or keep the enumeration to the two most common triggers with a note that more exist.

issues.statement: most teams use Slack (`type: 'slack'` with `conversationId` and `accessToken`)
issues.reason: The provided source only shows SlackIntegration as a type name in the AlarmUserIntegration union. The specific property names 'conversationId' and 'accessToken' are not visible in the provided alarms.d.ts source. While likely correct (and would be caught by the code-block-validator if in a CodeBlock), as a prose claim about specific property names it cannot be verified from the available source.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove the specific property names from the prose ('most teams use Slack or a custom webhook â€” see Alarms for channel configuration'), or leave as-is if the code-block-validator confirms these property names exist in the full type system.

evidence.quote:
type AlarmUserIntegration =
  | MsTeamsIntegration
  | SlackIntegration
  | EmailIntegration
  | DiscordIntegration
  | WebhookIntegration;

issues.statement: FlowDiagram component usage at the bottom of the page
issues.reason: The draft uses a <FlowDiagram steps={[...]} /> component. This component is not mentioned in the documented MDX component list visible in the instructions (which includes CodeBlock, ApiReference, ReferenceableParams, Info, Tip, FeatureComparisonTable). Without a definitive available components list, this may or may not be valid.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify that FlowDiagram is a registered MDX component in the docs framework. If not, replace with a simple bulleted list or use an alternative available component.

positiveFindings: All 9 Lambda deployment strategies (4 canary, 4 linear, AllAtOnce) are correctly enumerated and match the source LambdaDeploymentConfig.strategy union exactly.
All 15 guardrail types from guardrails.d.ts are accounted for in the table â€” none missing, none invented.
All 5 alarm notification channel types (Slack, MS Teams, Discord, email, webhook) correctly match the AlarmUserIntegration union.
Alarm evaluation properties (period, evaluationPeriods, breachedPeriods) and their defaults (@default 60, @default 1, @default 1) are accurately represented.
Budget features (scope per-stack vs organization, threshold, alertAtPercentages, includeForecast, alertChannels, forServices, forStages) all verified against BudgetsPage.tsx console source.
Gradual deployment ALB requirement correctly stated â€” source JSDoc confirms 'Requires loadBalancing type application-load-balancer'.
All cross-links (/cli/debug-logs, /cli/domain-add, /observability/alarms, /deployment-and-lifecycle/gradual-deployments, /guardrails/overview, etc.) point to valid routes in the navigation index.
Code examples use correct class names from the validated whitelist (LambdaFunction, WebService, StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging, StacktapeImageBuildpackPackaging).
No forbidden patterns detected: no hand-authored YAML, no StacktapeConfig type usage, no fenced TypeScript for config blocks, no FAQ section (correct for Getting Started pages).
Custom domains requirement for Route53 hosted zone correctly stated, matching the JSDoc.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the supplied alarm, web-service, Lambda deployment, budget, and guardrail sources, and it avoids the forbidden Stacktape config formats. The main factual risks are a few Stacktape-specific claims that go beyond the provided source files, especially SQS alarm wording, custom-domain breadth, DNS automation, and gradual deployment rollback details.

issues.statement: â€śAlarm triggers cover ... SQS queue depth.â€ť
issues.reason: The supplied alarm types do not expose an SQS queue-depth threshold trigger. They expose `sqs-queue-received-messages-count` and `sqs-queue-not-empty`; the latter fires when several queue activity metrics are non-zero, not when a configurable queue-depth threshold is crossed.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace â€śSQS queue depthâ€ť with â€śSQS received-message count and not-empty alarms,â€ť or describe the exact two supported SQS trigger types.

evidence.quote:
The queue is considered "not empty" if any of these are non-zero: visible messages,
   * in-flight messages, messages received, or messages sent.

issues.statement: â€śOther public resources (Lambda functions with URLs, hosting buckets, SSR frontends) have their own domain configurationâ€ť and â€śStacktape supports disabling automatic DNS record creation.â€ť
issues.reason: The provided source files only ground `customDomains` for `WebServiceProps`, where Stacktape auto-creates DNS records and TLS certificates. The supplied Lambda source shows `url` and `cdn`, but no custom-domain property, and no supplied source documents disabling automatic DNS record creation.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow this section to web services, or write cautiously: â€śOther public resources are covered in the custom domains guideâ€ť without listing unsupported resource types or DNS-disable behavior unless the relevant source files are provided.

evidence.quote:
* #### Custom domains (e.g., `api.example.com`). Stacktape auto-creates DNS records and TLS certificates.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Your domain must be added as a Route53 hosted zone in your AWS account first.
   */
  customDomains?: DomainConfiguration[];

issues.statement: â€śContainer workloads support a similar set of canary and linear deployment strategiesâ€ť and the named list â€śweb services, private services, worker services.â€ť
issues.reason: The supplied source verifies Lambda strategy values and verifies that a web service has a `deployment?: ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig` requiring application-load-balancer load balancing. It does not include the container deployment strategy union or the private-service and worker-service types, so the draft overstates what is grounded by the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Say: â€śWeb services expose container gradual deployments through `deployment`, and the source requires `application-load-balancer`; see the gradual deployments page for the container strategy list.â€ť Avoid naming private and worker services unless their source is included.

evidence.quote:
* #### Gradual traffic shifting for safe deployments (canary, linear, or all-at-once).
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Requires `loadBalancing` type `application-load-balancer`.
   */
  deployment?: ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig;

issues.statement: â€śIf alarms fire during the traffic shift, CodeDeploy rolls back automaticallyâ€ť and â€śIf either hook signals failure, the deployment rolls back.â€ť
issues.reason: The Lambda deployment source documents general gradual traffic shifting and explicitly says the before-traffic hook rolls back if it fails. The after-traffic hook source only says it must signal success/failure, and the provided files do not document alarm-triggered rollback wiring.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: â€śStacktape documents automatic rollback for gradual Lambda deployments, and the before-traffic hook rolls back if it fails. Use the gradual deployments page for the full rollback and alarm behavior.â€ť

evidence.quote:
* #### Function to run before traffic shifting begins (e.g., smoke tests).
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Must signal success/failure to CodeDeploy. If it fails, the deployment rolls back.
   */
  beforeAllowTrafficFunction?: string;

* #### Function to run after all traffic has shifted (e.g., post-deploy validation).
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Must signal success/failure to CodeDeploy.
   */
  afterTrafficShiftFunction?: string;

issues.statement: â€śCustom domains add no Stacktape cost, but Route53 charges ~$0.50/month per hosted zone plus a small per-query fee.â€ť
issues.reason: No pricing source is provided for Route53 or Stacktape custom-domain costs. The draft includes a concrete dollar amount and a Stacktape cost claim that are not grounded in the supplied files.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the specific price and say: â€śCustom domains can add Route53 hosted-zone and DNS-query charges in AWS.â€ť Only include exact pricing if a pricing source is provided.

evidence.quote:
* #### Custom domains (e.g., `api.example.com`). Stacktape auto-creates DNS records and TLS certificates.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Your domain must be added as a Route53 hosted zone in your AWS account first.
   */
  customDomains?: DomainConfiguration[];

issues.statement: â€śEach region gets its own independent stage â€” deploy with `--region eu-west-1` and `--region us-east-1` as separate commands or CI pipelines. Stacktape does not automatically replicate data between regions.â€ť
issues.reason: The supplied source list contains no multi-region deployment source or CLI command source. The guidance may be correct, but it is not grounded in the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Make this section more cautious or rely on the linked multi-region page: â€śUse the multi-region deployments guide for the exact deployment commands and data-replication expectations.â€ť

positiveFindings: All Stacktape configuration examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and `defineConfig`; no hand-authored YAML or `StacktapeConfig` plain-object style appears.
The Lambda deployment strategy list matches the supplied `LambdaDeploymentConfig.strategy` union exactly.
The guardrail table matches the supplied `GuardrailType` union and related guardrail property comments.
The budget section is mostly grounded in `BudgetsPage.tsx`: it reflects organization vs per-stack scope, thresholds, alert percentages, forecast inclusion, project/stage filters, and alert channels.
The draft uses the documented `stacktape debug:logs` route link instead of inventing `stacktape logs`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft mostly works as a high-level Getting Started production checklist, and the Stacktape config examples follow the required TypeScript CodeBlock pattern. I found three completeness/example issues worth fixing before publication; the largest risk is that the custom-domain section narrows the feature to web services while the page itself says production hardening across multiple public resource types.

issues.statement: The Custom domains section gives a web-service-only configuration example after saying other public resources have their own domain configuration.
issues.reason: Because this Getting Started page is a production checklist rather than a WebService page, readers are likely to leave thinking custom-domain production hardening is demonstrated only for web services. The draft names Lambda URLs, hosting buckets, and SSR frontends, but does not show or clearly defer their configuration. This is a code-example demonstrativeness gap for a broad production hardening section.
issues.suggestedFix: Either make the section explicitly web-service-focused and say the example is only the common API case, or add a compact comparison/list that links each supported public resource type to its domain guide. Keep the current WebService example as the primary path if that is the intended 80 percent case.

evidence.quote: Other public resources (Lambda functions with URLs, hosting buckets, SSR frontends) have their own domain configuration â€” see [Custom domains](/resources/networking/custom-domains) for the full setup guide across all supported resource types.

issues.statement: â€śAlarm triggers cover the most critical failure modes: ... SQS queue depth.â€ť
issues.reason: The SQS alarm union in the source names `sqs-queue-received-messages-count` and `sqs-queue-not-empty`; it does not expose a trigger literally described as queue depth. â€śQueue depthâ€ť may be a reasonable operational concept, but it blurs two distinct Stacktape trigger variants and can make readers search for a non-existent `queue-depth` trigger.
issues.suggestedFix: Name the SQS variants in source terms: â€śSQS received-message count and not-empty alarms.â€ť If useful, add that the not-empty alarm treats visible, in-flight, received, or sent messages as activity.

evidence.quote: The queue is considered "not empty" if any of these are non-zero: visible messages, in-flight messages, messages received, or messages sent.

issues.statement: The Gradual deployments section documents Lambda strategies in detail but only says container workloads support â€śa similar setâ€ť of strategies.
issues.reason: The draft documents the container workload deployment feature and says it supports canary and linear strategies, but does not name the actual container strategy variants or provide a container example. Under the union-variant and per-variant rules, a section that covers both Lambda and containers should give containers enough treatment for readers to decide and configure the feature, especially because the draft also states a load-balancing requirement.
issues.suggestedFix: Either narrow the section to Lambda and link containers to the gradual-deployments page, or add a container workload example with `loadBalancing: { type: 'application-load-balancer' }` and `deployment`, plus a concise list of supported container strategies from the container workload source.

evidence.quote: Container workloads support a similar set of canary and linear deployment strategies. Container gradual deployments require `application-load-balancer` load balancing.

positiveFindings: The page preserves the Getting Started flow and ends with follow-up links rather than trying to become an exhaustive reference page.
Every Stacktape configuration example uses `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and class-based `defineConfig`; there are no hand-authored YAML Stacktape config examples.
The alarms section covers the major alarm trigger families visible in `alarms.d.ts` and the example actually demonstrates `alarms`, `trigger`, `evaluation`, and `notificationTargets`.
The budgets section is aligned with the Console source: scope, threshold, alert percentages, project/stage filtering, forecast inclusion, and alert channels are all represented.
The guardrails section names all guardrail types from the provided `GuardrailDefinition` union at least once.
